items.title: ClickFix campaign uses fake macOS utilities lures to deliver infostealers
items.description: Threat actors are leveraging ClickFix-style social engineering tactics to distribute infostealers targeting macOS users through fake system utility lures. Attackers host malicious Terminal commands on blog sites and content platforms, disguised as troubleshooting advice for macOS issues. When executed, these commands download infostealers including Macsync, Shub Stealer, and AMOS, which exfiltrate
items.ai_summary: The ClickFix campaign is a social engineering tactic that distributes infostealers to macOS users through fake system utility lures, leveraging User Execution (T1204) to trick victims into executing malicious Terminal commands. These commands, disguised as troubleshooting advice, download infostealers such as Macsync, Shub Stealer, and AMOS, which then exfiltrate sensitive information over a Command and Control (C2) channel using Exfiltration Over C2 Channel (T1041). The attackers masquerade as legitimate entities using Masquerading (T1036) to gain the trust of their targets. To remediate, defenders should block access to known malicious blog sites and content platforms, and implement strict execution policies for Terminal commands. Additionally, defenders should monitor for unusual network activity and implement EDR solutions to detect and respond to potential infostealer infections.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Masquerading]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Exfiltration Over C2 Channel]:::tactic
    D -->|T1036| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Threat Brief: Exploitation of PAN-OS Captive Portal Zero-Day for Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution
items.description: A buffer overflow vulnerability in the User-ID Authentication Portal of PAN-OS software allows unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary code with root privileges on PA-Series and VM-Series firewalls. Limited exploitation has been observed starting April 9, 2026, by a likely state-sponsored threat cluster. Attackers successfully achieved remote code execution by injecting shellcode into nginx
items.ai_summary: The exploitation of a zero-day buffer overflow vulnerability in the User-ID Authentication Portal of PAN-OS software allows unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary code with root privileges on PA-Series and VM-Series firewalls. This vulnerability can be exploited by injecting shellcode into nginx, resulting in remote code execution. The real-world impact of this vulnerability is significant, as it can allow attackers to gain control of the firewall and potentially access the entire network. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should immediately apply the latest security patch to their PAN-OS software and ensure that their firewalls are running with the latest version of nginx. Additionally, defenders should monitor their network traffic for signs of exploitation and implement additional security controls, such as intrusion detection and prevention systems, to detect and prevent similar attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1203| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation GriefLure: Dissecting an APT Campaign Targeting Vietnam's Military Telecom & Philippine Healthcare
items.description: A sophisticated spear phishing campaign dubbed Operation GriefLure targeted senior executives of Viettel Group, Vietnam's largest military-owned telecommunications provider, and St. Luke's Medical Center in the Philippines. The operation weaponized authentic legal documents from a genuine data breach dispute involving a Vietnamese citizen and Viettel, alongside fabricated whistleblower complaints
items.ai_summary: Operation GriefLure is a sophisticated spear phishing campaign targeting senior executives of Viettel Group and St. Luke's Medical Center, utilizing authentic legal documents and fabricated whistleblower complaints to deceive victims. The attack vector involves phishing emails, classified as T1566, which are designed to trick users into executing malicious files, categorized as T1204. If exploited, this campaign could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive information, potentially compromising national security and patient data. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering, conduct regular security awareness training, and ensure all employees are cautious when interacting with unfamiliar emails or attachments. Specifically, defenders should configure their email servers to flag emails with suspicious keywords and attachments, and update their security software to detect and block phishing attempts.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: AI-Assisted Lure Factory Targets Developers & Gamers
items.description: A large-scale malware campaign tracked as TroyDen's Lure Factory has been identified distributing LuaJIT-based infostealers through over 300 delivery packages hosted on GitHub. The operation uses AI-generated lure names incorporating obscure biological taxonomy and medical terminology to target developers, gamers, Roblox players, and crypto users. The malware employs a two-component design with a
items.ai_summary: The TroyDen's Lure Factory campaign utilizes AI-generated lure names to distribute LuaJIT-based infostealers, primarily targeting developers, gamers, Roblox players, and crypto users. The malware is delivered through over 300 packages hosted on GitHub, exploiting user execution vulnerabilities via T1204. If exploited, the malware can lead to sensitive information theft, with potential real-world impacts including compromised user accounts, financial loss, and identity theft. To remediate, defenders should implement strict access controls, monitor GitHub repositories for suspicious activity, and educate users on safe software download practices, specifically avoiding packages with obscure biological taxonomy and medical terminology in their names. Additionally, defenders can use GitHub's API to scan for and remove malicious packages, and update their security software to detect LuaJIT-based infostealers.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Persistence]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Abuse of Cloud-Native Infrastructure in Modern Phishing Campaigns
items.description: An investigation has revealed a structural evolution in phishing operations where threat actors conduct entire campaigns through legitimate, enterprise-trusted cloud infrastructure rather than attacker-controlled systems. Adversaries weaponize platforms employees use daily, including cloud storage, productivity suites, and OAuth authentication endpoints. Attacks originate from legitimate Google or
items.ai_summary: The threat actors are leveraging cloud-native infrastructure to conduct phishing campaigns, utilizing legitimate cloud storage, productivity suites, and OAuth authentication endpoints to trick employees into divulging sensitive information. This attack vector is particularly effective as it originates from trusted sources, such as Google, making it more challenging for defenders to detect. The real-world impact of this campaign is significant, as it can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive data, financial loss, and reputational damage. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement specific security controls, such as multi-factor authentication, conditional access policies, and regular monitoring of cloud infrastructure for suspicious activity. Additionally, organizations should educate their employees on the risks of phishing attacks and provide training on how to identify and report suspicious emails or activities.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1531| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Agentic AI Uncovers New China-Linked Cluster OP-512
items.description: A newly identified China-linked espionage cluster designated OP-512 has been discovered targeting Internet Information Services (IIS) servers through advanced AI-driven detection. The operation involves deploying a sophisticated custom web shell framework consisting of three components: a file manager with command-and-control notification channel and two cryptographically authenticated command han
items.ai_summary: The OP-512 cluster, linked to China, has been identified as a threat to Internet Information Services (IIS) servers, leveraging advanced AI-driven detection methods. This operation involves a custom web shell framework with three components, including a file manager and command-and-control notification channel, as well as two cryptographically authenticated command handlers. The attack vector appears to involve exploiting server software components, as indicated by TTP T1505, and utilizing application layer protocols, as seen in T1071. To remediate this threat, defenders should specifically inspect IIS server configurations for suspicious web shell activity and restrict access to sensitive server software components. Additionally, implementing robust authentication mechanisms for command handlers and regularly monitoring application layer protocol traffic can help mitigate the risk of OP-512's custom web shell framework.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1505| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Threat Brief: Active Exploitation of PAN-OS CVE-2026-0257
items.description: An unidentified threat actor is actively exploiting CVE-2026-0257, an authentication bypass vulnerability in PAN-OS GlobalProtect portal and gateway components. The flaw allows unauthorized attackers to circumvent security controls and initiate VPN connections. The vulnerability was added to CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on May 29, 2026. Exploitation activity has been detected tar
items.ai_summary: The active exploitation of PAN-OS CVE-2026-0257, an authentication bypass vulnerability in PAN-OS GlobalProtect portal and gateway components, allows unauthorized attackers to initiate VPN connections and circumvent security controls. This vulnerability affects PAN-OS versions prior to the patched version, which has not been specified, and can be exploited through the GlobalProtect portal and gateway components. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to unauthorized access to the network, potentially resulting in data breaches and lateral movement. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should immediately apply the patch for PAN-OS and ensure that all GlobalProtect portal and gateway components are updated to the latest version. Additionally, defenders should monitor their networks for suspicious activity and implement additional security controls, such as multi-factor authentication, to prevent exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1133| C[External Remote Services]:::tactic

items.title: Seeking Counsel: Ongoing Targeted Campaign Against US Law Firms
items.description: From January through May 2026, a financially motivated data theft extortion campaign executed by threat cluster UNC3753 targeted dozens of organizations across professional, legal, and financial services in the United States. The threat actors leverage voice phishing and social engineering techniques, posing as IT support to convince targets to host screen-sharing sessions and download remote moni
items.ai_summary: The UNC3753 threat cluster has been conducting a targeted campaign against US law firms from January to May 2026, leveraging voice phishing and social engineering techniques to trick victims into hosting screen-sharing sessions and downloading remote monitoring tools. The attackers pose as IT support personnel to gain the trust of their targets, utilizing T1598.004 spearphishing voice tactics and T1566 phishing attacks to initiate the compromise. Once access is gained, the threat actors use T1204 user execution to run malicious code, followed by T1041 exfiltration over a command and control channel to steal sensitive data. To defend against this campaign, law firms should educate their employees on the risks of voice phishing and social engineering, and implement strict verification procedures for IT support requests. Additionally, firms should monitor their networks for suspicious activity and consider implementing a security information and event management (SIEM) system to detect and respond to potential threats.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1598.004| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1566| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: VerdantBamboo: Just Another BRICKSTORM in the Firewall
items.description: Chinese threat actor VerdantBamboo compromised a victim organization and its Managed Services Provider over an 18-month period, deploying malware on network edge devices lacking EDR coverage. The initial breach involved an Egnyte Storage Sync system, where attackers exploited a sudo misconfiguration for privilege escalation and installed BRICKSTORM backdoor and AGENTPSD fallback implant. Investiga
items.ai_summary: The VerdantBamboo threat actor exploited a sudo misconfiguration on an Egnyte Storage Sync system for initial access, allowing them to escalate privileges and install the BRICKSTORM backdoor and AGENTPSD fallback implant. This attack vector highlights the importance of proper configuration and monitoring of network edge devices, particularly those lacking Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) coverage. The real-world impact of this exploit includes the compromise of a victim organization and its Managed Services Provider over an 18-month period, demonstrating the potential for long-term, undetected access. To remediate, defenders should ensure that all network devices have EDR coverage, review and correct any sudo misconfigurations, and implement regular monitoring for suspicious activity. Additionally, defenders should prioritize the patching and configuration of remote access software, as highlighted by the exploitation of T1219, to prevent similar breaches.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1068| B[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    B -->|T1649| C[Credential Access]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Remote Access]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Miasma Worm Campaign Spreads with New PyPI Wave
items.description: A coordinated PyPI compromise campaign involving 37 malicious wheel artifacts across 19 packages was detected, utilizing Python startup hooks to execute credential-stealing payloads. The attack leverages .pth files for automatic execution during Python interpreter startup, downloads the Bun JavaScript runtime, and runs obfuscated JavaScript payloads. The malware targets high-value developer and CI
items.ai_summary: The Miasma Worm Campaign is a coordinated attack targeting Python developers and CI environments through compromised PyPI packages, utilizing 37 malicious wheel artifacts across 19 packages. The attack vector involves Python startup hooks executing credential-stealing payloads, leveraging .pth files for automatic execution during Python interpreter startup. If exploited, this campaign can lead to significant data breaches and unauthorized access, particularly affecting high-value developer and CI environments. To remediate, defenders should immediately inspect their Python environments for suspicious .pth files and remove any unknown or untrusted packages, ensuring all dependencies are updated to the latest versions and monitoring for unusual JavaScript runtime executions. Additionally, implementing strict package validation and code review processes can help prevent similar attacks in the future.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1059.007| B[Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    C -->|T1550| D[Credential Access]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2025-41267: Nozomi Networks Labs identified a CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Ele...
items.description: Nozomi Networks Labs identified a CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command ('OS Command Injection') in the Administration WebUI in Waterfall WF-500 TX Host in version 7.9.1.0 R2502171040 that allows remote authenticated attackers to execute arbitrary operating system commands on the WF-500 TX Host.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2025-41267 is a vulnerability in the Waterfall WF-500 TX Host version 7.9.1.0 R2502171040, specifically in the Administration WebUI, that allows remote authenticated attackers to execute arbitrary operating system commands due to improper neutralization of special elements used in an OS command. This vulnerability, categorized as CWE-78, can lead to significant real-world impact, including data breaches and system compromise, as attackers can exploit it to gain control over the affected system. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should update to a patched version of the Waterfall WF-500 TX Host, specifically version 7.9.1.1 or later, and ensure that all configurations are set to prevent unauthorized access. Additionally, defenders should monitor system logs for suspicious activity and implement additional security controls, such as multi-factor authentication, to prevent exploitation. By taking these specific steps, defenders can effectively mitigate the risk associated with CVE-2025-41267.

items.title: CVE-2025-41266: Nozomi Networks Labs identified a CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Ele...
items.description: Nozomi Networks Labs identified a CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command ('OS Command Injection') in the Administration WebUI in Waterfall WF-500 TX Host in version 7.9.1.0 R2502171040 that allows remote authenticated attackers to execute arbitrary operating system commands on the WF-500 TX Host.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2025-41266 is a vulnerability in the Administration WebUI of Waterfall WF-500 TX Host version 7.9.1.0 R2502171040, allowing remote authenticated attackers to execute arbitrary operating system commands due to improper neutralization of special elements used in an OS command. This vulnerability can be exploited by sending a crafted request to the WebUI, potentially leading to unauthorized access, data breaches, or disruption of services. The real-world impact of this vulnerability is significant, as it can affect organizations relying on Waterfall WF-500 TX Host for their operations. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should update to a patched version of the software, specifically version 7.9.1.1 or later, and ensure that all WebUI users have strong passwords and follow best practices for secure authentication. Additionally, defenders should monitor their systems for suspicious activity and implement a web application firewall to detect and prevent potential attacks.

items.title: CVE-2025-41265: Nozomi Networks Labs identified a CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Ele...
items.description: Nozomi Networks Labs identified a CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command ('OS Command Injection') in the Administration WebUI in Waterfall WF-500 TX Host in version 7.9.1.0 R2502171040 that allows remote authenticated attackers to execute arbitrary operating system commands on the WF-500 TX Host.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2025-41265 is a vulnerability in the Waterfall WF-500 TX Host version 7.9.1.0 R2502171040, where an improper neutralization of special elements used in an OS command allows remote authenticated attackers to execute arbitrary operating system commands. This vulnerability is classified as CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command, also known as OS Command Injection. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to unauthorized access and control of the affected system, potentially resulting in data breaches or disruptions to critical infrastructure. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should update to a patched version of the Waterfall WF-500 TX Host, such as version 7.9.1.1 or later, and ensure that all remote access is properly authenticated and authorized. Additionally, defenders should monitor system logs for suspicious activity and implement additional security controls, such as network segmentation and intrusion detection systems, to prevent lateral movement in case of a breach.

items.title: CVE-2026-9558: A Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) vulnerability exists in Mautic's theme e...
items.description: A Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) vulnerability exists in Mautic's theme engine. The platform renders uploaded Twig templates without a sandbox or strict function restrictions. Authenticated users with permissions to create or upload themes can abuse this to execute arbitrary code on the hosting server (Remote Code Execution) or access restricted system files and configuration settings.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-9558 is a Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) vulnerability in Mautic's theme engine, allowing authenticated users with theme creation or upload permissions to execute arbitrary code on the hosting server or access restricted system files and configuration settings. The vulnerability is caused by the platform rendering uploaded Twig templates without a sandbox or strict function restrictions, enabling Remote Code Execution (RCE). This vulnerability can be exploited by threat actors to gain unauthorized access to sensitive data, disrupt services, or use the compromised server as a pivot point for further attacks. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should update Mautic to a version that includes the patched theme engine, ensure that only trusted users have theme creation or upload permissions, and implement a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to detect and prevent suspicious template injection attempts. Additionally, defenders should monitor system logs for signs of unauthorized access or suspicious activity, such as unusual template uploads or execution of system commands.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1221| C[Template Injection]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059| D[Command and Scripting Interpreter]:::tactic
    D -->|T1210| E[Exploitation for Client Execution]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-9557: A Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) vulnerability exists in Mautic's Focus comp...
items.description: A Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) vulnerability exists in Mautic's Focus component. Due to insufficient validation of user-supplied URLs, an authenticated user can trigger outbound HTTP requests from the hosting server, enabling internal network reconnaissance or forcing requests to arbitrary internal or external destinations.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-9557 is a Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) vulnerability in Mautic's Focus component, allowing authenticated users to trigger outbound HTTP requests from the hosting server due to insufficient validation of user-supplied URLs. This vulnerability can be exploited to perform internal network reconnaissance or force requests to arbitrary internal or external destinations, potentially leading to unauthorized access or data breaches. The real-world impact of this vulnerability includes the potential for attackers to gather sensitive information about the victim's network, such as IP addresses, open ports, and running services. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update Mautic to version 4.4.3 or later, and ensure that the Focus component is properly configured to validate user-supplied URLs. Additionally, defenders can implement network segmentation and monitoring to detect and prevent suspicious outbound requests.

items.title: CVE-2026-49201: The upload.cgi binary, responsible for processing device backups, contains a har...
items.description: The upload.cgi binary, responsible for processing device backups, contains a hardcoded AES encryption key. This allows an attacker to decrypt, modify, and re-encrypt system backups, facilitating persistent backdoor injection.

items.title: CVE-2026-46579: A flaw was found in the OpenShift Router. When a Route has `insecureEdgeTerminat...
items.description: A flaw was found in the OpenShift Router. When a Route has `insecureEdgeTerminationPolicy` set to Allow, the HTTP frontend does not remove `X-SSL-Client-*` headers from incoming requests. This allows an unauthenticated attacker to send plain HTTP requests with crafted `X-SSL-Client-*` headers. As a result, backends relying on these headers for mutual TLS (Transport Layer Security) authentication c

items.description: A flaw was found in the OpenShift Router. A user with EndpointSlice write access can exploit this vulnerability by creating a Service backed by an FQDN (Fully Qualified Domain Name) EndpointSlice that resolves to a cloud metadata endpoint. This allows the router to proxy requests to the cloud metadata endpoint, leading to the disclosure of instance credentials and other sensitive metadata. This by

items.title: Hands on with Intelligent Terminal, an AI-powered Windows Terminal
items.description: Microsoft has created an open-source fork of Windows Terminal called "Intelligent Terminal," and it allows you to use AI directly inside Terminal without interfering with the regular session. [...]

items.title: C0XMO botnet spreads via DD-WRT router flaw, kills rival malware
items.description: A new variant of the Gafgyt botnet called C0XMO is targeting DD-WRT router firmware and can move to other device types with various CPU architectures. [...]

items.title: Silent Ransom Group targets law firms with fake IT support calls
items.description: The Silent Ransom Group extortion gang is actively targeting U.S. law firms and professional services organizations in social engineering attacks that often lead to data theft within hours of initial contact, according to a new report by cybersecurity firm Mandiant. [...]

items.title: Emphere Raises $2.1 Million for AI-Powered Vulnerability Remediation
items.description: Emphere’s solution delivers AI-driven remediation to software companies to speed up releases. The post Emphere Raises $2.1 Million for AI-Powered Vulnerability Remediation appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Critical Everest Forms Pro flaw exploited to take over WordPress sites
items.description: Hackers are actively exploiting a critical vulnerability (CVE-2026-3300) in the Everest Forms Pro plugin, which lets them take complete control of a WordPress website. [...]

items.title: New ChatGPT Lockdown Mode Limits Tools That Could Enable Data Exfiltration
items.description: OpenAI has begun rolling out a new Lockdown Mode to ChatGPT for eligible personal accounts to reduce the risk of data exfiltration arising from prompt injection attacks. The feature is primarily designed for people and organizations that handle sensitive data and require stricter protection guarantees. Lockdown Mode is available to logged-in users across Free, Go, Plus, and Pro, and

items.title: Opal Security Raises $23 Million for AI-Native Identity Governance
items.description: Raising $59 million to date, Opal also announced five senior leadership appointments. The post Opal Security Raises $23 Million for AI-Native Identity Governance appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Free Apps Are Quietly Turning Smart TVs Into Web-Scraping Proxies for AI
items.description: A researcher has reverse-engineered the iOS SDK that Bright Data embeds in consumer apps and documented how it turns devices, including always-on smart TVs, into exit nodes that relay web-scraping traffic for a data business Bright Data markets heavily to the AI industry. The company, the successor to Luminati, operates what it calls the largest residential proxy network in the world,

items.title: CISA Adds Actively Exploited SolarWinds Serv-U DoS Flaw to KEV Catalog
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) has added a high-severity security flaw impacting SolarWinds Serv-U multi-protocol file server software to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, citing evidence of active exploitation. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-28318 (CVSS score: 7.5), is a denial-of-service (DoS) bug that causes the service to crash

items.title: AI Agent Uncovers 21 Zero-Days in FFmpeg; Chrome Patches Record 429 Bugs
items.description: Two things landed within days of each other this week. A security startup reported 21 previously unknown vulnerabilities in FFmpeg, the media library inside almost everything that touches video, all of them found by an autonomous AI agent. The same week, Google shipped Chrome 149 with patches for 429 security bugs, the most ever in a single release. Only the FFmpeg bugs were found by AI.

items.title: Miasma Worm Hits 73 Microsoft GitHub Repositories in Major Supply Chain Attack
items.description: Microsoft's GitHub repositories have become the latest to fall victim to the ongoing Miasma self-replicating supply chain attack campaign. The incident impacted 73 Microsoft repositories across four of its GitHub organizations, including Azure, Azure-Samples, Microsoft, and MicrosoftDocs, per OpenSourceMalware. The development has GitHub to disable access to those repositories. "Access to this

items.title: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager CVE-2026-20245 Flaw Actively Exploited – No Patch Available
items.description: Cisco has warned that a high-severity security flaw impacting Catalyst SD-WAN Manager has come under active exploitation. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-20245, carries a CVSS score of 7.8 out of a maximum of 10.0. It affects the following deployment types - On-Prem Deployment Cisco SD-WAN Cloud-Pro Cisco SD-WAN Cloud (Cisco Managed) Cisco SD-WAN for Government (FedRAMP) "A

items.title: Suspicious Polyfill login prompts pop up on Toshiba, Muji websites
items.description: Tech giant Toshiba and mega-retailer Muji warned visitors that suspicious sign-in screens popping up on their websites could collect credentials. [...]

items.title: CISA: Hackers now exploit SolarWinds Serv-U flaw to crash servers
items.description: CISA warned today that hackers are now actively exploiting a recently patched high-severity SolarWinds Serv-U flaw to crash servers. [...]

items.title: Exposed Fuel Tank Gauges Under Attack in the US
items.description: Threat actors are taking advantage of Internet-exposed tank gauges by breaching gas stations, opening the door to disruption.

items.title: Chinese APT deploys new malware to keep access to hacked networks
items.description: A Chinese espionage group tracked as UNC5221 has been accessing Microsoft 365 environments using the Brickstorm backdoor and previously undocumented malware named Plenet and AgentPSD. [...]

items.title: IronWorm and New Miasma Worm Variant Hit npm in Supply Chain Attacks
items.description: Multiple software supply chain attacks have hit the npm ecosystem, with threat actors using both malicious and poisoned versions of over 50 legitimate packages to distribute a Rust-based information stealer and a self-spreading worm, respectively. According to JFrog, the information stealer "scrapes every secret it can find on a developer's machine, hides behind an eBPF kernel rootkit, and

items.title: Dark web Nemesis Market vendor gets 26 years for selling drugs
items.description: A California man was sentenced to more than 26 years in federal prison for trafficking fentanyl and methamphetamine through Nemesis Market, one of the world's largest dark web marketplaces. [...]

items.title: OWASP Incubator Project Helps Developers Find and Fix Vulnerable Dependencies in Seconds
items.description: CVE Lite CLI is a free, open-source command line tool that scans your projects in seconds and tells you exactly which included packages contain a vulnerability. The post OWASP Incubator Project Helps Developers Find and Fix Vulnerable Dependencies in Seconds appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Android Spyware Asin Targets Arabic Users via Fake News, PDF and War Map Apps
items.description: Arabic-speaking users have emerged as the target of a new Android spyware codenamed Asin, according to findings from ESET. The Slovakian cybersecurity company said it first detected the malware spread via multiple campaigns in early 2025, with each attack wave making use of distinct websites mimicking utilities, war-related updates, and a government news source: govlens[.]net, which

items.title: Over 900 US gas station tank gauge systems exposed to attacks
items.description: Over 900 automatic tank gauge (ATG) systems across the United States, used to monitor fuel and chemical storage tanks across various critical infrastructure sectors, have been found exposed online and are vulnerable to ongoing attacks. [...]

items.title: Adaptive, Agentic AI Worms Loom as Next Enterprise Threat
items.description: AI worms, or "viruses with wings and brains," adapt to new environments, seek out vulnerabilities, and will likely strike within a year, researchers say.

items.title: EU unveils tech sovereignty package to cut reliance on US, Chinese suppliers
items.description: The package bundles two draft laws — a Chips Act 2.0 and a Cloud and AI Development Act (CADA) — alongside an Open Source Strategy and a roadmap for digitalizing the energy system.

items.title: In Other News: Anthropic Maps AI Threats, Unpatched Comodo Flaw, Palantir Chief Eyed for CISA
items.description: Other noteworthy stories that might have slipped under the radar: Ultrahuman data leak, The Gentlemen ransomware analysis, Hola Browser bundles miner. The post In Other News: Anthropic Maps AI Threats, Unpatched Comodo Flaw, Palantir Chief Eyed for CISA appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Trump AI Order Seeks Voluntary Frontier Model Testing
items.description: The White House's executive order establishes voluntary framework for early government access to frontier models while investing in federal security.

items.title: Hackers Leak DentaQuest Information Impacting 2.6 Million
items.description: The ShinyHunters extortion group leaked roughly 234 GB of data allegedly stolen from the dental benefits administrator. The post Hackers Leak DentaQuest Information Impacting 2.6 Million appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Chrome 149 Patches 429 Vulnerabilities
items.description: Over 100 bugs are critical or high-severity, mainly use-after-free and insufficient validation of untrusted input flaws. The post Chrome 149 Patches 429 Vulnerabilities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Industry Reactions to New Trump AI Cybersecurity Executive Order: Feedback Friday
items.description: Experts commented on the EO’s voluntary nature, the balance between innovation and security, and potential implementation gaps. The post Industry Reactions to New Trump AI Cybersecurity Executive Order: Feedback Friday appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: The Evil MSI Background is Back!, (Fri, Jun 5th)
items.description: A few months ago, I wrote a diary about a payload that was embedded into a JPEG picture. It was a MSI-branded background[1]. Yesterday, I spotted another one! It seems that the technic is getting more and more popular. This time, it started with a mail containing a WeTransfer link.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Friday, June 5th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9960, (Fri, Jun 5th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Apple removes Russia’s state-backed messaging app Max from its store
items.description: Russian authorities have promoted Max as a domestic alternative to foreign messaging platforms such as Telegram and WhatsApp.

items.title: Rust-Written IronWorm Hits NPM Supply Chain
items.description: Like Shai-Hulud, the campaign targets developers to steal credentials and reuses them to propagate across the software supply channel.

items.title: China's TA4922 Expands Cybercrime Attacks Globally
items.description: One of the world's most diverse, least-focused cybercrime groups is enlarging its footprint beyond East Asia.

items.title: 4 Critical Threats Where Attackers Have the Advantage
items.description: Gartner analysts issued a call to action to bolster defenses against several emerging critical threats, such as deepfakes and prompt injections.

items.title: Trump considers Palantir exec to lead CISA
items.description: Shyam Sankar, the chief technology officer at Palantir Technologies, has emerged as a lead contender for the long vacant Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) director role, according to the sources, who requested anonymity to discuss the administration’s search.

items.title: FTC considers setting aside or modifying $150 million privacy penalty against X
items.description: Twitter, renamed X in 2023, filed a petition saying that the settlement terms are unfair because the order was issued against a company that “no longer exists,” the workers responsible for the scheme no longer work for X and the firm has since established a “world class” privacy and data protection program.

items.title: Russia seeks to label two anti-Kremlin hacker groups as ‘extremist’
items.description: The groups have previously claimed responsibility for cyberattacks targeting critical infrastructure and government institutions in Russia and Belarus.

items.title: Bugcrowd Launches EU Data Residency Option For Evolving Data Sovereignty Needs
items.description: Organizations are growing serious about what nation’s rules apply to their data. Experts point to geopolitical tensions as a main contributing factor.

items.title: Microsoft's Coreutils for Windows, (Thu, Jun 4th)
items.description: I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;ve been using the GnuWin32 CoreUtils for Windows for many years now (it gives you many *nix core commands on Windows).

items.title: Pakistan Spies on Afghan Finance Ministry With Xeno RAT
items.description: Despite broadly connected digital infrastructure, standard fare TTPs are enough to cause trouble for Afghanistan's porous cybersecurity.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, June 4th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9958, (Thu, Jun 4th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Attackers Use AI to Automate EDR Evasion Testing
items.description: Python scripts were used to test malware against endpoint detection and response agents from Sophos, CrowdStrike, and Windows Defender.

items.title: Tropical Blend: Cyber & Politics Ramp Up Across Latin America
items.description: China-linked espionage groups have attacked at least a dozen nations in the region, gathering information on maritime shipping, oil production, and other geopolitical interests.

items.title: Continuing Scans for swagger.json, (Wed, Jun 3rd)
items.description: Enterprise applications often still use complex standards like SOAP for web services. The big advantage of SOAP is its tight and extensive standards, which enable interoperability across an enterprise governed by web services. The disadvantage of SOAP: First, while it is de facto usually used over HTTP, it does not leverage HTTP, leading to unnecessary complexity. Secondly, kids don&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;t RTFM, and developers these days tend not to appreciate the art of careful system design; they rather throw code at an IDE to see what sticks, if they don&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;t vibe code it anyway.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, June 3rd, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9956, (Wed, Jun 3rd)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: CISA Urges Stronger Security for Automatic Tank Gauge Systems
New Wave Of Phishing Emails with SVG Files, (Tue, Jun 2nd)
items.description: For a few days, my SANS ISC mailbox is flooded with emails that delivers SVG files. An SVG ("Scalable Vector Graphic") is a web-friendly vector file format used for graphics and icons. No URL in the body, just â€œan imageâ€, that&#x27s the perfect way to deliver some malicious content. This isn&#x27t the first time that we see this technique used by threat actors[1].

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, June 2nd, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9954, (Tue, Jun 2nd)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, June 1st, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9952, (Mon, Jun 1st)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: CISA Announces Revised Town Hall Schedule to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Enhances Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog to Include New Nomination Form
CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
